item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
kinda horrifying to be one of the folks trapped. im surprised they couldnt like, airdrop supplies or something for these people.

what happens if you get a package including some horse pill to knock them out and a sledge attached to a rope leading out of the anomaly next to them in some loophole bullshit (CATAPULT) way? can you drag somebody who holds no intent to go anywhere out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a soft spot for Keters that are not inherently dangerous, shorter articles, and anomalies that involve spatial fuckery. This is well-written, interesting, and checks all those boxes. Plus to the one.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open - Attention!
item.content:encoded: The wikiwalk/crosslink timer is running out! Don't miss out on the opportunity to help shape the wikiwalk! Arm your headcanons! Discuss!!!!

item.title: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

They only build these walls...
...to keep us apart.

Alright, so as far as function is concerned, this is remarkably similar to SCP-2449, and as each can spontaneously manifest, it seems like a no-brainer that they could be linked (out-of-universe). So, I'd like to propose the following crosslink:

Additionally, 2 more affected locations were subsequently identified, centered around a small municipal park in ████████, Australia and an abandoned warehouse in █████ ███████, Spain.

to

Additionally, 2 more affected locations were subsequently identified, centered around a small municipal park in ████████, Australia and an abandoned warehouse in █████ ███████, Spain. A third location which appeared on this date was found to be an instance of SCP-2449. The significance of its concurrent manifestation is unknown.

ETA: I'd also like to link to SCP-2728 in some way, due to them being unapproachable locations, but I can't think of a non-intrusive way to do it. Open for suggestions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But the people deeper in the affected area were surrounded by newly unapproachable areas.

OHHHHHHHH I GET IT BETTER NOW

those boundaries can be something like "the telephone poles overhead define a discrete airspace location" which the pedestrian now can't approach.

YOU SLY DOG YOU :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
^This

I wish that we could upboat comments so that I would do that instead of what some might consider shitposting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think what he means is that if they get big enough to cover the entire Earth, it won't matter. Although I will say that it would make launching things into space impossible D:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this SCP, but the addendum feels like it's just been tacked on for the sole purpose of making it Keter. Other than that, I love this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think a disinformation campaign would be able to fool the local governments. I think you'd need to replace the government employees who deal with these sorts of things with foundation agents, and have the embedded agents lie to the rest of the government.

There are various ways to spread disinformation and yes, planting agents in the local governments is one of those ways. And for the most part, city governments tend to not investigate private properties unless there's a reason to do so. So long as the Foundation pays the appropriate property taxes and doesn't cause any complaints to be filed with the city, the properties will likely stay off the radar for quite a while.

Are the blockages real, or just perceived? And if they're real, do they just appear out of nowhere, or does the anomaly rewrite the past in order to ensure they exist at the right moments?

A little of column A, a little of column B. The Foundation suspects that the SCP is doing some retrocausality probability manipulation, but can't get enough evidence to prove it one way or the other.

Example: Person A is driving next to Person B. A is trying to get into the parking lot of the affected hotel with the intent of going inside, while B is trying to get into the same parking lot with the intent of walking to the restaurant next door. A gets accidentally blocked by traffic and other drivers not letting him get into the correct lane, and 10 minutes later when he finally does get into the correct lane and circles back around, the car in front of him blows a tire, blocking the entrance to the parking lot. B, on the other hand, was able to get into the parking lot easily, find a spot within 100 feet of the restaurant, and walk over there, away from the hotel, with ease. Remember that it's possible to get to immediately adjacent areas; intent is pretty key here. Of course, if on the way back to his car, B decided to enter the hotel for whatever reason, he might trip over his shoelace that had come untied and fall, hitting his head pretty badly, so he decides to go to the hospital instead to get stitched up.

In other words, the blockages appear to be natural coincidences (for the most part), even if they're weird ones, but in aggregate they prevent anyone from actually getting to the affected areas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: hello and welcome to my gta experience.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Local disinformation campaigns are to be maintained in all cities hosting an instance of SCP-1698, with the intent to make all local residents or governmental officials believe that the locations are abandoned, closed for renovation, or otherwise off-limits.

I don't think a disinformation campaign would be able to fool the local governments. I think you'd need to replace the government employees who deal with these sorts of things with foundation agents, and have the embedded agents lie to the rest of the government.

Although they can be seen, all attempts to reach them by foot or land vehicle result in encountering obstacles such as intervening buildings, extensive road damage and attendant construction, gridlocked traffic, dead-ends and road closures, and thick and impassable vegetation.

Are the blockages real, or just perceived? And if they're real, do they just appear out of nowhere, or does the anomaly rewrite the past in order to ensure they exist at the right moments?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tell me about it. If I can, and it's someplace important, I walk there a day or so before, just to make sure I can find the place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's why, whenever possible, I leave 30 minutes early to go anyplace I've never been before. "Getting lost" time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given whenever I go somewhere that I wasn't to before, I'm constantly nervous about not actually going the proper way and not getting there? This is properly scary. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very interesting. My one question is, in light of the expansion event, has the storage command been rescinded?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very original, and I can imagine it would be pretty damn tragic to get stuck in these areas. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Which is exactly what happened with the expansion event and, again, the Foundation is likely removing those files from the buildings now that they're upgraded to Keter. Or at least trying to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And why is this a better idea than renting a non-anomalous building?

And this SCP is not fully understood. It could potentially develop some additional effect that would put all the data at risk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No the rules didn't change. If someone had been in a location on the periphery of the new area, they'd've been able to leave. But the people deeper in the affected area were surrounded by newly unapproachable areas. As for the inability to be rescued from the air, I'm basing at least part of this SCP on the psychological concept of "boundary events." Very basically, the mind uses physical boundaries as signs to end/create discrete purely mental boundaries as well. This is a leading hypothesis for why it's so easy to walk into a new room and completely forget why you went in there: The mind "closed" the thought when you crossed through the doorway and "started" a new one.

In the case of this SCP, something considered arbitrary boundaries as inviolate, separating discrete physical spaces. And those boundaries can be something like "the telephone poles overhead define a discrete airspace location" which the pedestrian now can't approach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The one thing I don't get is I thought you could /leave/ affected areas, but not re-enter them. Did the rules change with the expansion?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess I'm just a sucker for those "wait, what?" addendums that kind of explain it, but I see what you're going for with this, and I overall like the idea. I'm gonna up vote it ^^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A big part of the reason that this is Keter is because there's no explanation. If the Foundation had even a thread on which to pull to start explaining this, they'd be using it to try to stop/reverse expansion events.

By the time the Addendum was added, yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, but I feel like you could've added something that would give some sort of explanation, even if it isnt clear or doesnt make any actual sense. I dunno, no vote for now.

Also, are the people trapped in the buildings dead?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If there's a building that no one can reach, fuck it, now it's a storage site

Pretty much.

First of all, the approval was tentative/provisional, so they're ready to immediately pull records out of there if it proves to be a bad idea (and they might be doing that now that it's upgraded to Keter).

Second of all, all the data is extremely non-critical: they're old records and none deal with actual SCP data or documentation. It's like how, before digital records, hospitals would have warehouses full of paper files on patients that were seen once a decade ago and never since. The chances of anyone needing this data RIGHT NOW!!! is extremely slim, and while it would be annoying if it became totally inaccessible, it wouldn't be a major blow to the Foundation's infrastructure. More akin to losing your car insurance card and having to order a new one.

Third, these are just paper files. If/as they're digitized, they're removed from the buildings, and there are no data servers on-site. Even experimental data about SCP-1698 itself is uploaded off-site by wi-fi or satellite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I utterly failed to get a vibe of it trying to be humorous, because there's nothing "zany" about using an unreachable site to store information. It makes logical sense.

It's like downvoting for "LOL, those zany security personnel and their willingness to provide mercy!" in the containment procedures. It's just an aspect of how the Foundation does their job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I saw this as the Foundation utilizing all the resources at hand. If there's a building that no one can reach, fuck it, now it's a storage site. I don't think it was intended to be funny but another peek into the inner workings of the Foundation.

I was on the cusp of upvoting until I read the addendum. Short and sweet. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted for this line;

and O5 command has tentatively approved the usage of these sites for long-term storage of infrequently accessed non-digital financial and administrative paperwork.

Using an SCP to store paperwork is just cliche "LOL, those zany bureaucrats and all their paperwork!" humor, and not very good humor at that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's not really anything else the Foundation can do to contain the phenomenon. It's sorta self-containing in that people outside can't make their way in. Foundation efforts are dedicated to making sure that people don't have any reason to try to get there, so they won't notice that they can't.

But SCP-1698 has now demonstrated the ability to unpredictably increase in size and number, and permanently trap people caught in it. If the Foundation had some way to prevent the expansion events, it'd likely be Safe. But since they can't, it's Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not that bad, but it needs more, like maybe an interview with someone who was in the building when the Foundation found it. Or maybe a log of some long term effects of being stuck in an area. It just needs more of an edge. And for Keter, it seems a little to simple/short.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not really. Keep in mind that the people are all in the same geographical region, but trapped in different locations. There are people stuck on a 50' length of sidewalk, unable to leave. There are people trapped in a 7-11 because they can't quite reach the doors out. There are people driving who can't leave the local road grid because all exits are blocked. The inability to approach affected locations isn't that big a deal when you're outside looking in, but when you're inside and stuck in-between multiple areas that you can't reach, well, you're kinda SOL.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems to me that if this gets big enough, it actually becomes mostly harmless again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see anything wrong with a simple Keter. It's still dangerous and dificult to contain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It seems awfully short and simple for a keter. I'm not sure if that's a bad thing or not. I'll hold off on a vote for now, but some more in-depth details might improve this.

Don't get me wrong, I think the concept is pretty interesting and the execution isn't bad. It's something I can relate to. It's just… Really brief. Needs more containment description I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, but that one is a sentient/sapient being that alters its interior topography at will, for fun. Or, you know, to punish people who vandalize it.

EDIT: SCP-413

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've had the whole "Building is RIGHT THERE but I can't get to it!" sort of thing happen to me before. +1 for somehow making it an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't we already have a parking complex that sends you in a loop forever?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a picture that I might add, depending on how well this does before I get home.
